title: The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.
caption: Across countries in 2023, the burden of disease measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) per 100,000 people shows a negative relationship with GDP per capita. The scatter plot places DALYs on the vertical axis and GDP per capita on a logarithmic horizontal axis, with circle sizes representing population. Countries in sub-Saharan Africa cluster in the upper-left quadrant, indicating high disease burden and low income; the Central African Republic, Niger, Chad, and Burundi all register above 60,000 DALYs per 100,000 with GDP per capita below $3,000. In contrast, high-income nations in North America, Europe, and parts of Asia occupy the lower-right, with the United States, Germany, Japan, and Singapore all below 30,000 DALYs and GDP per capita exceeding $40,000. Large-population countries like India, Indonesia, and China appear as oversized circles in the middle range, with DALYs between 20,000 and 40,000 and GDP per capita from $5,000 to $20,000. The pattern visible is a downward slope from left to right: as GDP per capita increases, DALYs per 100,000 tend to decrease.
